content.full_text:
SH
2A
INC.
ARCHITECTS

Jerry M. Schroeder, AIA
Jay V. Hyde, AIA

1718 Third Street, Suite 201
Sacramento, California 95814
916/441-0686
916/325-4838 FAX

April 21, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

We recently read an article in the CASH Register, the newsletter
of Coalition for Adequate School Housing (CASH), an organization
to which we belong. The article, copy enclosed, says that you
have determined that architects under contract with local school
districts to provide architectural and engineering services for
school projects, working under the standard State Allocations Board
contracts, are considered "public officials". Therefore, you have
determined that we are obligated to file economic interest
disclosure statements (FPPC form 730), disclosing our personal
finances and that we are subject to the Political Reform Act's
conflict of interest provisions.

The article goes on to say that we could have a conflict-of-
interest that may preclude us from signing a change order or to
recommend a contractor...

We don't understand!

o    How can we become a "public official" when we have been
retained under contract to provide a professional
service for an agreed-upon price, particularly since our
selection process was an open public process to
determine the best qualified firm?

o    How can we have a conflict of interest administering a
construction contract with a contractor with whom we had
worked on previous projects as the architect?

Could you please provide us a copy of your internal memos and an
outline of your reasoning that describes how you made this
determination. We need to understand what the issue really is and
if and why we are obligated to provide financial disclosure.

Could you please provide us with FPPC form 730.

We fail to understand how you could interpret that working under
our contracts (the standard OLA version that almost every
architect that designs schools works under) could put us in a
conflict-of-interest situation and would obligate us to provide
financial disclosure.

Further, since we provide architectural services for the state and
many other public jurisdictions, we are concerned that this
reasoning will suddenly expand to other clients and beyond us to
our subcontractors.

We are looking forward to hearing from you.

Jerry M. Schroeder, AIA
Architect/President

JMS:lr

cc:\file\FPPC.1

March 1992                                                                                                    CASH Register Page 5

"Ruling on Disabled," continued from page 4

The problem with large-scale mainstreaming, officials said, is that districts cannot afford to pay for the aides and consultants — probably special-education teachers — required for full inclusion of developmentally disabled students. "Common sense obviously tells you cost is going to be a factor," Royston said. "Before, you could use a special day class to provide education for up to 12 students. Now, you have to have teachers travel from school to school to provide services for these children one-on-one."

In addition, many teachers will find it difficult to give lessons to 30 or more students in a regular classroom and also find time to help a developmentally disabled student learn, said Plodkin, a former board member of Protection and Advocacy Inc., a federally funded, state corporation that advocates for developmentally disabled people.

"For anyone to say that can be done easily is dealing in abstractions," said Plodkin, who added that he was both "exuberant" and "demoralized" by the possible implications of Levi's ruling.

In his decision, Levi called district officials' cost estimates for placing Rachel in a regular classroom "excessive and hyperbolic." During a December hearing in the case, California Department of Education officials testified that any additional costs for full inclusion of Rachel or similarly disabled students would be minimal.

The 9-year-old's parents have told her little about the federal case involving her education, Connor said.

"It's really hard to tell her that, of all places, a school district doesn't want her to go to school with her friends," the mother said.

"Whether someone doesn't want your child because of your race, ethnicity, gender or disability, it has the same impact on your family."


ARCHITECTS TAKE NOTICE!

The Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) staff has recently concluded that architects retained by school districts and the State Allocation Board to build new schools under the standard State Allocation Board contract are "consultants" who are "public officials" under the Political Reform Act. This means the individual architects within the architectural firm who function as architects of record on a new school must file annual economic interest disclosure statements (FPPC form 730) to publicly disclose their personal finances. The architect is also subject to the Political Reform Act's conflict-of-interests. In the future, the architects may be unable to sign a change order, or recommend a contract for whom the architect's firm has done previous work, without finding himself or herself subject to possible prosecution by the FPPC for conflict-of-interest.

This FPPC interpretation problem arose when an attorney for a school district telephoned a friend on the FPPC staff to ask whether the disclosure and conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply when architects work on public school projects. He was advised "yes" — an architect working under contract with a public agency is a "public official." As customary, the staff made a written, internal (but precedent-setting) memorandum of this advice for their files.

A letter was submitted arguing that an architect who works under a standard contract to build a new school is not a "public official" and should not be required to file disclosure forms or be subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. The FPPC staff disagreed.


STATE ALLOCATION BOARD ACTIONS

The State Allocation Board at its monthly meeting on March 25 took the following actions.

■ Asked staff to develop a draft policy for the exclusion from "adequate area" for purposes of eligibility for child care and pre-school programs.

■ Approved staff's recommendation to continue to approve projects without apportionments so that districts may continue to move their projects forward prior to state funding.

■ Supported staff's recommendation to deny an appeal from the year-round education requirement which was brought to the Board by the Valley Center Union School District. The Waiver and Review Committee is comprised of Lyle Smoot, Assistant Executive Officer, State Allocation Board; and Leroy Small, Consultant, School Facility Planning Division, California Department of Education.

The district argued that it would be difficult to implement year-round education, and that such a program would increase busing costs and promote segregation. Certain board members were sympathetic to the district's request and spoke against the overall concept of year-round education and its impact on students. The Board ultimately agreed to uphold the staff's recommendation and denied the request.

■ The Board approved a Revised Reimbursement/Funding Policy which staff explained was necessitated by new Federal Treasury Department Regulations. This is a highly complex issue, one which the Office of Local Assistance Staff will attempt to explain in a series of workshops which will be held in various locations over the next few weeks. (This issue was not published in time to include a schedule of such meetings).
